q: Genie SQL expressions have three types: measures, filters, and fields. What is each for?
a: SQL expressions are reusable, named business-concept definitions you add to a Genie Space so Genie uses precise SQL whenever a user references that concept. The three types are: (1) Measures — KPIs and aggregated metrics, such as 'gross_margin' defined as SUM(revenue - cost) / SUM(revenue). These are the core calculation layer: they capture how a metric is computed so Genie never re-derives it differently on each question. (2) Filters — boolean conditions that represent a common data slice, such as 'high_value_orders' defined as order_total > 1000. Users can reference them by name and Genie applies the exact WHERE clause. (3) Fields (dimensions) — attribute expressions that transform or rename a column for a business concept, such as 'deal_size' derived as CASE WHEN arr < 10000 THEN 'SMB' WHEN arr < 100000 THEN 'MM' ELSE 'ENT' END. These three types map to the three core query patterns: aggregate (measure), filter (filter), group by (field). The SQL is written exactly as Genie will emit it, so correctness is auditable. Unlike text instructions, SQL expressions are deterministic — Genie applies them verbatim rather than interpreting natural language guidance.
fj: I'll put all my business definitions in the Instructions text box — it's one place to explain everything.
fs: Text instructions are interpreted by the LLM and can drift. SQL expressions are applied verbatim: measures define aggregations exactly, filters define exact WHERE clauses, fields define exact GROUP BY / CASE expressions. Use Instructions only when structured SQL can't express the guidance.
